title: Quantum Forge Pilot Project with IonQ in Quantum Computing or Quantum Sensing

advisor.name: IonQ quantum industry technical lead and Quantum Forge or Q-SEnSE affiliated mentor

coadvisors_raw: A William & Mary Physics professor in quantum computing or quantum sensing will serve as co-advisor once the final technical direction is set.
pitch: Quantum is no longer just basic science. It is now an applied, engineering-driven field where teams build devices, control systems, algorithms, and measurement platforms that matter outside the lab. This proposed Quantum Forge capstone with IonQ puts students inside that shift. You would join a year-long project in either quantum computing or quantum sensing, work directly with a quantum industry technical lead, and help shape solutions to a real problem emerging from the quantum technology sector. If you want a capstone that feels close to where advanced engineering is headed rather than where physics used to be, this is that project.
background_objective_deliverables:
This proposed capstone project is part of Quantum Forge, a year-long capstone model developed by NSF Q-SEnSE to engage undergraduate students in real-world quantum information science and technology projects. For the 2026-2027 William & Mary Engineering Physics capstone cohort, this project is being developed with IonQ as a pilot industry collaboration. IonQ works in areas connected to quantum computing and quantum sensing, and the final project scope is now being defined in conversation with the company and program mentors.

The project is expected to fall in one of two applied directions: quantum computing or quantum sensing. In either case, students will not be joining an abstract theory exercise. They will be working in a part of quantum technology that increasingly looks like modern engineering: defining requirements, comparing architectures, modeling system behavior, evaluating hardware or software constraints, building and testing workflows, and communicating results to technical stakeholders. Quantum technologies now depend on engineers who can connect physics fundamentals to design decisions, instrumentation, computation, controls, and performance tradeoffs. This project is meant to give students that experience directly.

Students who choose this project will be directly mentored by an IonQ quantum industry technical lead together with Quantum Forge or Q-SEnSE affiliated mentorship and a William & Mary Physics co-advisor whose expertise matches the final topic area. Depending on scope, student work may include literature and market landscape review, problem framing, simulation and modeling, algorithm or measurement workflow development, experimental design, prototype or proof-of-concept development, benchmarking, data analysis, and technical reporting.

Likely deliverables include a technical background review, a clearly defined problem statement with requirements, intermediate design or progress reviews, and a final report and presentation. Depending on the selected topic, the team may also produce a simulation tool, analysis pipeline, prototype design, experimental plan, sensing workflow, or proof-of-concept demonstration. The goal is to give students meaningful exposure to how quantum technologies are actually advanced today: through interdisciplinary engineering work grounded in physics, but aimed at practical performance and real-world use.
thrust_area: Quantum science and technology, Computational imaging / simulation, Instrumentation / sensors
lab_workspace_access: Primary work will be based in William & Mary Physics and associated engineering spaces, with regular interaction with external mentors. Specific laboratory, computational, or instrumentation access will depend on whether the final project centers on quantum computing or quantum sensing.
notes: This is a proposed pilot Quantum Forge project for the 2026-2027 capstone cycle. The exact topic is still being finalized with IonQ and program mentors. Primary advisor is the IonQ quantum industry technical lead together with a Quantum Forge or Q-SEnSE affiliated mentor. A William & Mary Physics professor in the relevant area will serve as co-advisor.
